_comment: This file is NOT used directly by Docker Compose.
It is the request body you POST to Debezium's REST API.
See: scripts/register-connector.sh for how to register it.
WHY a REST API instead of a config file?
Kafka Connect is designed to run connectors dynamically.
You can add, remove, update, pause, and resume connectors
without restarting any container. This is how production
CDC systems work — connectors are managed at runtime.
'name'   → unique identifier for this connector instance
name: postgres-cdc-connector

config._section_connector_class: --- What type of connector is this? ---
config._section_connector_identity: --- Connector identity (affects topic names and offset keys) ---
_comment_topic_naming: topic.prefix controls how Kafka topics are named.
config.slot.name: debezium_syncstream_slot
config.plugin.name: pgoutput
_comment_plugin: plugin.name='pgoutput' is PostgreSQL's built-in logical decoding plugin.
Available since PostgreSQL 10. No extra installation needed.
Alternative: 'decoderbufs' (requires a PostgreSQL extension to be installed).
We use pgoutput because it ships with PostgreSQL out of the box.
_comment_table_include: Whitelist approach: only named tables are captured.
Alternative: table.exclude.list (blacklist — capture everything EXCEPT listed tables).
Whitelist is safer in production: new tables don't accidentally get captured.
_comment_snapshot_mode: 'initial'   → Snapshot existing data on first run, then stream changes.
On subsequent runs (restart), skip snapshot and resume streaming.
This is the standard production setting.
'always'    → Snapshot ALL data on EVERY restart.
Causes duplicate 'r' events in Kafka on each restart.
Only use if you need to rebuild consumers from scratch regularly.
'never'     → Skip snapshot entirely. Only stream future changes.
Use after initial snapshot is complete and you're iterating.
'exported'  → Advanced: snapshot from a known PostgreSQL backup.
We use 'initial' so that downstream systems (Redis, ES) are bootstrapped
with existing data before streaming begins.
_comment_tombstones: tombstones.on.delete=true means that for every DELETE event,
1. The delete event: key={id:1}, value={op:'d', before:{...}}
Kafka log compaction removes old messages for the same key,
keeping only the latest value. Without a tombstone, the last
event for a deleted row would be the DELETE event — keeping it
forever. A null tombstone signals to Kafka: 'this key is gone,
you can remove all history for it.'
We check op='d' to trigger deletion in Redis/ES.
We IGNORE tombstones (null values) — they are for Kafka's benefit.
_comment_decimal: PostgreSQL NUMERIC/DECIMAL types are arbitrary precision.
Kafka/JSON doesn't have an arbitrary precision type.
'precise'  → Encoded as bytes (exact, but Java needs special handling)
'double'   → Converted to Java double (slight precision loss for large values)
'string'   → Encoded as string (safe, but consumers must parse)
We use 'double' for simplicity during learning.
For financial data in production: use 'string' or 'precise'.
Our prices are small enough that double precision is fine.
_comment_time: PostgreSQL TIMESTAMPTZ → how to encode in Kafka events.
We use 'connect' during learning because ISO-8601 is readable
in the Kafka UI without mental epoch conversion.
_comment_performance: max.batch.size: maximum number of events Debezium reads from WAL at once.
max.queue.size: internal buffer size between WAL reader and Kafka publisher.
poll.interval.ms: how often to check for new WAL entries (milliseconds).
These are sensible defaults for development.
In production at high throughput: increase batch sizes,
tune poll.interval.ms based on acceptable latency.
_comment_heartbeat: Problem: If a table has very few writes, Debezium doesn't advance
its WAL position for a long time. PostgreSQL cannot clean up old WAL
because the replication slot position hasn't moved.
Result: WAL disk space fills up, database crashes.
Solution: Debezium writes a heartbeat event to a special Kafka topic
every 10 seconds (10000ms). This causes the WAL position to advance
even when there are no data changes.
Consumers should ignore events on this topic.
